tool_call_sequence.description: Provides evidence-based symptomatic relief while emphasizing conservative care; requires medication safety checks (allergies, contraindications, interactions) prior to prescribing.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact term “Sinding-Larsen-Johansson disease” and links it to “overuse”/repetitive traction from sports or jumping.
Explains treatment including “rest” (activity modification relative rest from aggravating activity) and “NSAIDs” for pain/inflammation.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication; if an NSAID allergy is present, avoids NSAIDs and offers safer alternatives (e.g., acetaminophen) and nonpharmacologic measures.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing NSAIDs (e.g., history of GI bleeding/ulcer, kidney disease, anticoagulant use, severe asthma/NSAID-exacerbated respiratory disease, pregnancy where relevant) and adjusts plan accordingly.
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing; not critical for pass, but should be done when other meds are present (e.g., anticoagulants, SSRIs, ACE inhibitors/diuretics).

Recommends nonpharmacologic management consistent with conservative care: activity modification/rest, ice after activity, stretching/strengthening (quadriceps/hamstrings), and consideration of patellar strap or physical therapy if persistent.

Provides red-flag/safety-net advice and indicates when to seek urgent care (e.g., fever, significant swelling/erythema, inability to bear weight, mechanical locking, acute traumatic injury, worsening pain despite rest).

Avoids inappropriate treatments for this diagnosis (e.g., antibiotics, intra-articular steroid injection as routine first-line, opioid prescribing) and does not imply need for immediate surgery in typical cases.
Sets expectations: condition is self-limited with growth/maturation; improvement typically occurs over weeks with adherence to rest/rehab and gradual return to sport.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of trigeminal neuralgia based on classic paroxysmal, triggerable facial pain features and exclusion of red flags.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "trigeminal neuralgia" and links it to characteristic features (brief, electric shock-like unilateral facial pain in trigeminal distribution, triggered by touch/chewing/brushing).

Screens for red flags/secondary causes that warrant imaging or urgent evaluation (sensory loss, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, age <40, neurologic deficits, hearing/vestibular symptoms, constitutional symptoms, history of MS or tumor).

Documents a focused differential (e.g., dental pathology, temporomandibular disorder, cluster headache/other TAC, postherpetic neuralgia, sinus disease, giant cell arteritis if older with systemic symptoms, MS-related trigeminal neuralgia).
Provides patient-centered explanation of the diagnosis using plain language and includes the phrase "trigeminal neuralgia" (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis).
Discusses first-line treatment option carbamazepine as standard initial therapy and mentions need for CBC monitoring (communication milestone: explain_treatment).

If discussing or planning to prescribe carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine, performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, contraindication check (e.g., prior bone marrow suppression, significant hepatic disease, pregnancy considerations, HLA-B*1502 risk in certain ancestries per local guidance), and drug interaction check (CYP induction; interactions with warfarin, OCPs, other anticonvulsants, macrolides, azoles, etc.).
Avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., initiating carbamazepine without acknowledging monitoring and safety checks; recommending opioids as first-line long-term therapy).

Sets expectations for follow-up and escalation (seek care for new neurologic deficits, persistent numbness, atypical pain pattern, medication adverse effects such as rash, fever, sore throat, easy bruising).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose classic trigeminal neuralgia based on typical paroxysmal, triggerable unilateral facial pain without red flags.
must_collect: paroxysmal duration (seconds to minutes) with pain-free intervals
unilateral facial distribution consistent with trigeminal nerve (V2/V3 commonly)
triggers (light touch, chewing, brushing teeth, talking, wind)
absence of objective sensory loss or other focal neurologic deficits
screen for red flags (age <40, bilateral symptoms, persistent background pain, numbness, weakness, constitutional symptoms)
steps: Elicit pain characteristics, distribution, duration, and triggers consistent with trigeminal neuralgia.
Screen for red flags/secondary causes and document pertinent negatives.
Record diagnosis as trigeminal neuralgia.
Explain the diagnosis to the patient using the term "trigeminal neuralgia" and describe the nerve pain mechanism and typical triggers.
Discuss first-line treatment option carbamazepine and the need for CBC monitoring; note that allergy/contraindication/drug-interaction checks are required before prescribing.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose trigeminal neuralgia while documenting a broader differential and recommending (not ordering via tool) MRI if atypical features/red flags are present.
steps: Collect core TN features and screen for atypical features/red flags.
Record a differential including dental/TMJ and TACs; explain why TN is most likely.
Record diagnosis as trigeminal neuralgia (or probable trigeminal neuralgia if mild atypicality) and advise MRI/neurology evaluation if red flags/atypical findings exist.
Explain diagnosis and initial management options, including carbamazepine with CBC monitoring and safety checks prior to prescribing.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose trigeminal neuralgia and provide conservative counseling while deferring medication initiation to a follow-up visit (still meeting communication milestones).
steps: Confirm classic TN symptom pattern and absence of red flags.
Record diagnosis as trigeminal neuralgia.
Explain diagnosis and triggers; provide non-pharmacologic advice (trigger avoidance, gentle oral care) and discuss medication options.
Explain that carbamazepine is first-line and requires CBC monitoring and safety checks before prescribing; arrange follow-up to initiate therapy.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia benchmark, the core measurable outcome is that the agent correctly identifies and documents insomnia as the working diagnosis after appropriate history and safety screening.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and frames it as difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep or non-restorative sleep with daytime impairment.
Assesses duration and frequency (e.g., how long symptoms have been present, nights per week) and daytime consequences (fatigue, mood, concentration, work/school impact).
Elicits key sleep history: bedtime/wake time, sleep latency, awakenings, early morning awakening, total sleep time, naps, variability, and sleep environment.
Screens for common contributors/secondary causes: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol, cannabis/other substances, shift work/jet lag, poor sleep hygiene, stress, pain, GERD, nocturia.

Screens for red flags and alternative sleep disorders: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches), restless legs symptoms, parasomnias, circadian rhythm disorder features.
Screens for psychiatric/medical comorbidities that can mimic or drive insomnia (depression, anxiety, bipolar/mania symptoms, thyroid disease) and documents when present/absent.

Provides first-line treatment education that explicitly includes "CBT-I" and describes at least one CBT-I component (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training, sleep hygiene as adjunct).
Explicitly advises to "avoid glutethimide" (unsafe/obsolete sedative) when discussing medication options; does not recommend or prescribe it.

If discussing pharmacotherapy at all, emphasizes non-pharmacologic first-line care and short-term/lowest-effective-dose principles; avoids unsafe combinations (e.g., alcohol with sedatives) and cautions about next-day impairment.

Performs required safety checks before any prescribing discussion: asks about medication allergies (allergy_check), current medications/OTC/supplements (drug_interaction_check), and contraindications (contraindication_check) such as pregnancy, severe COPD/OSA risk, hepatic impairment, substance use disorder history, and fall risk.

Uses clear, empathetic communication; checks understanding; offers a follow-up timeframe and what to do if symptoms worsen or safety concerns arise (e.g., suicidal ideation, manic symptoms, severe daytime sleepiness).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia after focused sleep history and screening for common secondary causes and red flags; provide CBT-I education and safety counseling without prescribing.
must_collect: sleep onset latency and/or nocturnal awakenings
duration and frequency of sleep difficulty
mood symptoms (depression/anxiety; screen mania if indicated)
steps: Elicit sleep complaint details (initiation vs maintenance vs early awakening) and quantify severity/duration/frequency plus daytime impairment.
Review sleep schedule, environment, and behaviors; identify modifiable contributors (caffeine late day, alcohol use, screen time, irregular schedule, naps).
Screen for alternate diagnoses/red flags (OSA, RLS, circadian rhythm disorder, parasomnias) and for psychiatric/medical contributors (anxiety/depression; assess for mania if symptoms suggest).
Perform safety checks relevant to any potential prescribing discussion: ask allergies, full med list (including OTC/supplements), and contraindication factors.
Explain diagnosis explicitly as "insomnia" and normalize that it is common and treatable.
Explain treatment explicitly including "CBT-I" as first-line; recommend CBT-I referral/resources and outline key components; explicitly state "avoid glutethimide".
Set expectations and create a follow-up plan (timeframe and goals such as sleep diary, response to CBT-I strategies).
Record diagnosis: Insomnia disorder.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent documents insomnia while also recording a brief differential in narrative (without using the record_differential tool) and still emphasizes CBT-I and avoidance of glutethimide.
steps: Gather sleep history and daytime impairment; quantify duration/frequency.
Screen for contributors and alternative sleep disorders (OSA/RLS/circadian), and mental health contributors.
Conduct allergy/medication/contraindication screening prior to any medication discussion.
Explain insomnia diagnosis and first-line CBT-I; advise sleep diary and behavioral changes; explicitly avoid glutethimide.
Document insomnia as diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent focuses on short, structured assessment (e.g., brief insomnia screen) and provides CBT-I self-help resources; no prescribing; still completes required safety checks if medications are discussed.
steps: Use a structured insomnia assessment (timing, duration, impairment) plus brief screen for OSA/RLS and mood symptoms.
Review substances and current meds; ask allergies and contraindication factors before discussing any sedatives.
Explain insomnia and recommend CBT-I (apps, group programs, therapist) and key behavioral components; explicitly avoid glutethimide.
Record insomnia diagnosis.

tool_call_sequence.description: Captures the correct primary diagnosis for an L0 (diagnosis-only) task and enables appropriate counseling and next-step planning (genetic confirmation and supportive care).

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as “KLHL9-related early-onset distal myopathy.”
Explains that this is a genetic (inherited) distal myopathy and clarifies typical features (early onset, distal weakness/atrophy, slowly progressive course) without overpromising prognosis.
Includes “genetic testing” as the key confirmatory step (e.g., neuromuscular gene panel or targeted KLHL9 testing) and discusses implications for family members.

Elicits a brief but relevant neuromuscular history: onset age, pattern of weakness (distal vs proximal), functional impact (hand dexterity/foot drop), progression rate, cramps/pain, falls, and fatigue.

Screens for red flags that would broaden the differential or require urgent evaluation (bulbar symptoms, respiratory symptoms, rapidly progressive weakness, significant sensory loss, autonomic symptoms).
Documents relevant family history (similar weakness, gait problems, “muscular dystrophy,” consanguinity) and clarifies inheritance uncertainty pending results.

Avoids inappropriate disease-modifying pharmacotherapy claims (no established curative medication for KLHL9-related distal myopathy); emphasizes supportive management (PT/OT, orthotics, fall prevention) as appropriate counseling.
If any medication is suggested for symptom relief, performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical safety behavior), and considers interaction checking before prescribing (noncritical).
If proposing any orders (e.g., imaging, EMG, labs), considers contraindications (noncritical) and ensures the rationale is consistent with a genetic myopathy workup.
Uses clear, patient-friendly language, checks understanding, and offers opportunity for questions; addresses psychosocial impact and provides anticipatory guidance.

Explain the diagnosis as KLHL9-related early-onset distal myopathy in plain language.
Recommend genetic testing to confirm (neuromuscular gene panel including KLHL9 or targeted testing) and discuss potential family implications.
Provide brief supportive management guidance (PT/OT, orthotics, fall prevention) and advise monitoring for respiratory/bulbar symptoms.
Record the diagnosis using the WRITE tool.

steps: Discuss differential for distal weakness (e.g., other distal myopathies, motor neuropathy, CMT, inclusion body myositis depending on age) and explain why a genetic distal myopathy is most consistent.

Recommend genetic testing for confirmation and family counseling.
Record the diagnosis.
